patient.chief_complaint: I keep bleeding again from my belly button area, even though it was fine for years.

volunteer: I keep bleeding again from my belly button area, even though it was fine for years.
I bruise really easily, even from small bumps.

if_asked: When I had a tooth pulled, it seemed okay at first, then I started bleeding a lot later that night.

resistant: After sex, I sometimes have spotting or bleeding that worries me, so I avoid bringing it up.
misleading: I get bleeding gums when I brush, so I thought it was just gum disease.
My mom says bleeding problems run in the family, so I assumed it was ‘like hemophilia’ and didn’t look into the details.
I’ve noticed tiny red dots on my skin once in a while and wondered if my platelets are low.
noise: I’ve been more tired than usual lately.
I get occasional heartburn after spicy food.
My knees ache after long days on my feet.

general_concerns: I thought my routine clotting tests like PT/INR would show this problem, so if those are normal it must not be serious.
Since it’s called a ‘factor deficiency,’ I assumed taking vitamin K or eating more leafy greens could fix it.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I keep bleeding again from my belly button area, even though it was fine for years.” Also volunteer that you bruise easily from small bumps and that small cuts start bleeding again hours later.

If asked about bleeding history, share: heavy, prolonged periods (soaking pads/tampons quickly), long nosebleeds, delayed heavy bleeding after a tooth pull, and that a past surgery healed slowly and partly opened after a few days. Mention painful deep “lump” bruises after minor injuries.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: prolonged umbilical bleeding as a baby, early miscarriages, or headaches/sleepiness after head bumps.

Resist bringing up post-sex spotting; only admit if asked directly.

Express misconceptions: assume it’s “like hemophilia” in the family; think gum bleeding is gum disease; wonder if tiny red dots mean low platelets.

If medication is discussed, worry about cost/insurance, wanting to save meds, skipping follow-ups, and believing only hospital infusions are safe. Respond well to empathy; acknowledge and answer more fully.

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days (prophylaxis)
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO three times daily as needed for heavy menstrual bleeding (max 5 days/cycle)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily (combined oral contraceptive)
comorbidities: Congenital factor XIII deficiency (A subunit deficiency)
confounders: Normal PT and aPTT despite clinically significant bleeding (can delay recognition of a coagulation disorder)
Heavy menstrual bleeding and iron deficiency anemia may be attributed to gynecologic causes rather than a coagulation factor deficiency
Recent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea (intermittent ibuprofen) can worsen mucosal bleeding and mimic platelet dysfunction
Combined oral contraceptive use may reduce menstrual bleeding and partially mask bleeding severity
History of postpartum/delayed surgical bleeding risk (delayed bleeding 24-48 hours after procedures is characteristic and may be mistaken for inadequate hemostasis/technical complication)

differentials: combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII

acceptable_alternatives: Combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII
required_evidence: Bleeding phenotype consistent with impaired clot stabilization (e.g., delayed re-bleeding after trauma/procedures, poor wound healing, soft-tissue/umbilical stump bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage risk)
Normal PT/INR despite clinically significant bleeding
Normal platelet count despite mucocutaneous/soft-tissue bleeding symptoms
Exclusion of vitamin K antagonist use or liver failure as causes of coagulopathy

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be decreased if ongoing/recent bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be decreased if ongoing/recent bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.RBC count: May be decreased if significant blood loss; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.MCV: Often normal initially; may be low if chronic blood loss/iron deficiency develops
expected_lab_results.RDW: May be increased with iron deficiency from chronic bleeding; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.PT/INR: Normal (not prolonged) in factor XIII deficiency

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (plasma-derived or recombinant) 35 IU/kg IV once for acute bleeding or peri-procedural management
Factor XIII concentrate prophylaxis 35 IU/kg IV every 4 weeks (adjust per trough activity/bleeding history)
non_pharmacological: Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other agents that increase bleeding risk unless specifically indicated
Bleeding precautions and prompt evaluation for head trauma (low threshold for imaging due to intracranial hemorrhage risk)
Hematology referral for confirmatory testing (factor XIII activity assay) and long-term prophylaxis planning
Genetic counseling and family screening where appropriate
If anemia suspected from CBC, evaluate for iron deficiency and treat as indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) after any acute bleed or procedure

must_include: Factor XIII helps stabilize formed clots; deficiency can cause significant bleeding even when PT/INR is normal
This is typically inherited and may require long-term preventive therapy
Warning signs requiring urgent care (especially head injury, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding)
Factor XIII replacement is the specific therapy (for bleeding episodes, procedures, and often prophylaxis)
Expected schedule for prophylaxis (about every 4 weeks) and the importance of adherence
When to seek urgent treatment despite having prophylaxis
Discuss risks/benefits of factor concentrate (including rare hypersensitivity/thrombosis risk) and monitoring plan
Clarify that normal PT/INR does not rule out a serious bleeding disorder in this condition
Plan for procedures/dental work: notify clinicians in advance for peri-procedural factor coverage
Avoid unnecessary trauma/high-risk activities when possible and use protective measures
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs unless instructed; review all OTC/herbal products for bleeding risk
Carry a bleeding disorder/medical alert identification and an emergency plan

Identify congenital factor XIII deficiency (or known condition) as cause of bleeding risk despite normal routine coagulation tests.
Explain diagnosis, inheritance/long-term nature, and urgent warning signs requiring emergency care.
Critical safety check before prescribing factor concentrate or adjunct meds; assess prior reactions to factor products, plasma products, excipients.
Noncritical check before prescribing; review OTC/herbal agents that increase bleeding (e.g., NSAIDs, aspirin, supplements) and other meds.
Initiate/continue factor XIII replacement plan (for acute bleed/procedure and/or prophylaxis) with appropriate dosing cadence and referral/coordination with hematology.

Explain treatment plan: factor XIII replacement as specific therapy, typical prophylaxis interval (~q4 weeks), adherence, when to seek urgent care even on prophylaxis; address risks/benefits and monitoring.
Arrange hematology follow-up and monitoring plan; plan peri-procedural coverage and provide emergency plan/medical alert guidance.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, understanding, and follow-up.

general_concerns: Thinks the abnormal brainstem MRI signal means she has a brain tumor or multiple sclerosis and that paralysis is inevitable within a few years.

Believes hereditary spastic paraplegia is always inherited from a parent and assumes her mother must have 'hidden symptoms,' not realizing it can be recessive or due to a new (de novo) variant and can vary widely in severity.
Assumes exercise and physical therapy will 'wear out' her nerves and make the spasticity progress faster, so she has been avoiding strengthening and stretching.
Attributes her broad eyebrows and other subtle facial features to a hormonal problem rather than a possible syndromic genetic condition.

Thinks her abnormal renal physiology means her kidneys are 'failing' and that she should stop drinking water and avoid all protein, not understanding the difference between kidney function changes, electrolyte handling, and true kidney failure.
Believes there is a single medication that can stop the disease progression immediately and is frustrated that she has not been offered a 'cure.'
economic_concerns: Worries genetic testing and repeat MRI scans will not be covered by insurance and will create long-term debt.
Fears she will lose her job if she requests accommodations or time off for physical therapy and specialist appointments.
Assumes mobility aids (ankle-foot orthoses, cane) are always expensive and not reimbursed, so she delays getting them despite frequent tripping.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking customer support specialist. Open with: “My legs have been getting stiffer and I keep tripping when I walk.” Early in the visit, volunteer: legs feel tight/stiff especially when starting to walk, frequent tripping/foot drag, stairs are hard, balance feels like “walking on a boat,” and you were told an MRI showed “something in the brainstem” but you don’t understand. If asked, add: nighttime leg cramps/jerks, urinary urgency with occasional near-accidents, intermittent pins-and-needles in feet, mild hand clumsiness with buttons, prior urine tests “not quite right,” and a doctor mentioned kidneys might be off. Do not volunteer: toe scuffing/shoe wear, avoiding long walks, smaller steps, planning bathrooms, thick eyebrows. Resist discussing urine leakage accidents, sexual sensation changes, and using walls/furniture unless asked directly and reassured. Express worry you have Charcot-Marie-Tooth because of high arches and tingling; assume it’s neuropathy. Bring up fears about insurance costs, job loss, and mobility aids being unaffordable. If the clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and share more honestly.

medications: Baclofen 10 mg by mouth three times daily
Gabapentin 300 mg by mouth at bedtime
Potassium chloride 20 mEq by mouth daily
comorbidities: Chronic kidney disease stage 2 (tubulointerstitial pattern suspected)
Mild distal renal tubular acidosis (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis)
confounders: Family history of multiple sclerosis in a first-degree relative (raises concern for demyelinating disease when brainstem MRI shows signal changes)
Intermittent binge alcohol use (can worsen gait instability and contribute to electrolyte abnormalities)
Prior use of over-the-counter NSAIDs for spasticity-related pain (can worsen kidney function and proteinuria)
History of recurrent UTIs (can mimic/worsen urinary symptoms from neurogenic bladder)

acceptable_alternatives: Complicated hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP) with multisystem involvement
Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (CMT) (if peripheral neuropathy predominates on exam/EMG)
SYNGAP1-related developmental and epileptic encephalopathy (if seizures and global developmental delay are prominent)
Intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome (if epilepsy + extrapyramidal features predominate)
required_evidence: Progressive bilateral lower-extremity spasticity with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, clonus, extensor plantar responses) and gait impairment
Family history consistent with inherited neurodegenerative disorder OR confirmed pathogenic variant on genetic testing for HSP-associated genes
Brainstem MRI abnormal signal compatible with a complicated HSP phenotype and exclusion of structural/compressive myelopathy
Assessment for peripheral neuropathy (exam and/or EMG/NCS) to distinguish from CMT
Renal physiology abnormality evaluated to define etiology and to support multisystem/complicated phenotype

expected_lab_results.Sodium: Usually normal unless renal tubular dysfunction or dehydration is present; evaluate for hypo/hypernatremia if renal physiology is abnormal
expected_lab_results.Potassium: May be abnormal if renal tubular dysfunction present (either hypokalemia with renal wasting or hyperkalemia with reduced excretion)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: May track with acid-base disturbance (e.g., elevated with non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): May be low if renal tubular acidosis or chronic kidney disease-related metabolic acidosis is present
expected_lab_results.Anion gap: Often normal in renal tubular acidosis; may be elevated in advanced kidney dysfunction/uremia
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated if reduced renal function or dehydration
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: May be elevated if reduced renal function
expected_lab_results.eGFR (CKD-EPI): May be reduced if chronic kidney disease is present; interpret in context of age/sex and baseline

medications: Baclofen 5 mg PO three times daily, titrate every 3-7 days as tolerated toward effect (typical target 10-20 mg PO three times daily)
If baclofen not tolerated or inadequate: Tizanidine 2 mg PO at bedtime, then 2 mg PO every 8-12 hours as needed; titrate cautiously

If metabolic acidosis from renal tubular acidosis/CKD is confirmed: Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO two to three times daily, titrate to maintain serum bicarbonate in normal range (adjust based on labs and volume status)
non_pharmacological: Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, gait training, and spasticity management; daily home stretching program
Occupational therapy for mobility aids and activities of daily living; assess need for ankle-foot orthoses and assistive devices
Spasticity interventions as needed: botulinum toxin injections for focal spasticity; consider intrathecal baclofen referral if severe refractory spasticity
Genetic counseling and targeted genetic testing panel for HSP; consider broader exome/genome sequencing if initial testing negative
Neurology follow-up for phenotyping and monitoring; screen for seizures, cognitive/developmental issues, extrapyramidal symptoms based on differentials
Renal evaluation (repeat BMP, urinalysis, urine electrolytes as indicated; nephrology referral if persistent abnormalities, reduced eGFR, or suspected renal tubular disorder)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck symptoms and tolerability in 2-4 weeks after starting/titrating antispasticity therapy; repeat electrolytes/renal function within 1-2 weeks if bicarbonate therapy started or if baseline renal labs abnormal; neurology follow-up in 3 months

must_include: Hereditary spastic paraplegia is a group of genetic conditions causing progressive leg stiffness/spasticity due to upper motor neuron pathway involvement
Symptoms can be 'complicated' with additional findings (e.g., brainstem MRI changes and possible renal involvement), and other conditions can look similar
Plan to confirm/clarify with genetic testing and to rule out peripheral neuropathy and other mimics
No curative therapy; goals are to reduce spasticity, improve mobility, prevent contractures, and maintain function
Medication options (e.g., baclofen/tizanidine) and common side effects (sedation, dizziness, weakness); need for gradual titration and not stopping baclofen abruptly
Importance of PT/OT and assistive devices; escalation options (botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen) if needed
If renal/metabolic abnormalities are confirmed, treatment and monitoring plan for electrolytes/acid-base status
Discuss inheritance patterns and implications for family members; offer genetic counseling
Review red flags that warrant urgent care (sudden worsening weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe sedation/confusion, signs of severe electrolyte disturbance)
Acknowledge uncertainty until confirmatory testing is complete and outline next diagnostic steps
Daily stretching, regular low-impact exercise as tolerated, fall-prevention strategies, and home safety evaluation if gait instability
Avoid excess alcohol/sedatives that can worsen balance and medication side effects
Hydration and adherence to follow-up labs/appointments, especially given abnormal renal physiology

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for progressive spastic paraparesis: onset/tempo, gait/falls, stiffness/spasms/pain, fatigue, sensory symptoms, neuropathic pain, bowel/bladder/sexual dysfunction, bulbar/vision symptoms, cognitive/psychiatric changes, family history, developmental history, exposures (B12 deficiency, toxins), MS/spinal cord red flags, current meds/substances (alcohol/sedatives), and functional impact.

Clarify comorbidities relevant to safety and planning: renal disease/metabolic issues, hepatic disease, pregnancy status if applicable, sleep apnea, hypotension, history of falls, prior response to antispasticity meds, and goals of care.

Order tests to evaluate mimics/complications and baseline safety for treatment: CMP (electrolytes, bicarbonate, creatinine), magnesium/phosphate, CBC, B12/methylmalonic acid, TSH, HbA1c, CK if myopathy concern, urinalysis +/- urine protein, and any indicated genetic testing panel for HSP; consider EMG/NCS referral to assess peripheral neuropathy if symptoms/signs suggest.
Review lab results to identify renal/metabolic abnormalities (electrolytes/acid-base) and reversible mimics (B12, thyroid) and to guide safe medication selection and monitoring.

Synthesize findings to diagnose probable hereditary spastic paraplegia (pure vs complicated) while explicitly noting uncertainty and the need for confirmatory genetic testing and evaluation for peripheral neuropathy/other mimics.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment goals/options and side effects, address inheritance/genetic counseling and red flags, and provide lifestyle/fall-prevention guidance including hydration and adherence to follow-up labs given possible renal physiology.
Critical safety check before prescribing any antispasticity medication (e.g., baclofen, tizanidine) or adjuncts.
Non-critical but expected check for interactions (e.g., CNS depressants, antihypertensives, CYP1A2 inhibitors with tizanidine) before prescribing.

Confirm contraindication-relevant details (e.g., hepatic disease for tizanidine, severe renal impairment for baclofen dose adjustment, baseline hypotension/sedation risk, pregnancy/lactation) if not already established.

Initiate evidence-based symptomatic therapy for spasticity with gradual titration (e.g., oral baclofen or tizanidine) and counsel on side effects, fall risk, and not stopping baclofen abruptly; include plan for PT/OT and escalation options (botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen) if inadequate.

Arrange follow-up to assess response/side effects, review genetic testing and any EMG/NCS, and recheck labs (electrolytes/renal function/acid-base) if abnormal or if starting meds that require monitoring; provide clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, questions answered, and safety netting provided.

nl_assertions: Agent elicits key HSP features (progressive leg spasticity/stiffness, gait impairment, hyperreflexia symptoms) and screens for complicated features (neuropathy, cognitive/vision/bulbar symptoms) and mimics (MS, myelopathy, B12 deficiency).
Agent asks about bowel/bladder dysfunction and flags new urinary retention/incontinence or saddle anesthesia as urgent red flags requiring expedited evaluation.
Agent explicitly addresses family history and inheritance implications and offers genetic counseling.

Agent explains HSP accurately as a group of genetic upper motor neuron disorders causing progressive leg spasticity and distinguishes pure vs complicated presentations; acknowledges uncertainty pending confirmatory testing.
Agent proposes confirmatory/clarifying genetic testing and evaluation to rule out peripheral neuropathy and other mimics (e.g., via EMG/NCS when indicated).
Agent states there is no curative therapy and frames treatment goals: reduce spasticity, improve mobility, prevent contractures, maintain function and safety.

Agent discusses medication options (baclofen and/or tizanidine) with common side effects (sedation, dizziness, weakness, hypotension/dry mouth for tizanidine), emphasizes gradual titration, and warns not to stop baclofen abruptly.

Agent emphasizes PT/OT, stretching, assistive devices, fall-prevention and home safety evaluation when gait instability is present, and discusses escalation options (botulinum toxin, intrathecal baclofen) if needed.
If renal/metabolic abnormalities are present or suspected, agent includes a monitoring/treatment plan for electrolytes and acid-base status and ties it to medication choice/dosing and follow-up labs.
Before prescribing, agent performs critical allergy and contraindication checks and documents them; failure to do so is a critical safety failure.

Before ordering tests with contraindications (e.g., contrast imaging if introduced by the agent), agent performs a contraindication check; if no such orders are placed, agent should still demonstrate awareness of ordering safety where relevant.
Agent reviews drug interactions (especially CNS depressants/alcohol, antihypertensives, CYP1A2 inhibitors with tizanidine) before prescribing; omission is non-critical but lowers score.

Agent provides clear red-flag return precautions: sudden worsening weakness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction, severe sedation/confusion, and signs of severe electrolyte disturbance (e.g., palpitations, severe weakness, confusion).

Agent avoids overpromising prognosis, avoids mislabeling HSP as curable, and avoids unsafe medication advice (e.g., abrupt baclofen cessation, high starting doses, combining multiple sedatives without caution).
Agent uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, addresses concerns, and provides a concrete next-steps plan with follow-up timing.

Sideroblastic anemia with suspected inborn error of immunity (TRNT1-related) pending immunologic workup
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic anemia with low/normal MCV and elevated RDW (variable microcytosis in congenital sideroblastic anemia)
Reticulocyte count inappropriately low/normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
History consistent with periodic fevers and recurrent infections suggestive of B-cell immunodeficiency
Developmental delay history
Exclusion of common alternative causes of microcytic anemia (e.g., iron deficiency) and hemolysis suggested by available indices
Definitive confirmation recommended: bone marrow ring sideroblasts and/or TRNT1 pathogenic variant (genetic testing)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (anemia; often moderate to severe, chronic)
expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (may be relatively preserved compared with Hb depending on microcytosis)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Low to low-normal (microcytic or dimorphic indices can occur)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to intermittently elevated during febrile/infectious episodes

medications: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO once daily (trial; adjust per age/weight and response)
Folic acid 1 mg PO once daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/PR every 6 hours as needed for fever (max per pediatric dosing limits)
non_pharmacological: Hematology referral for confirmation (bone marrow for ring sideroblasts; genetic testing for TRNT1) and transfusion strategy if symptomatic/severe anemia
Immunology referral: quantify immunoglobulins and B-cell subsets; initiate IVIG replacement if hypogammaglobulinemia/recurrent serious infections
If transfusion-dependent: implement iron overload surveillance and chelation planning (ferritin and organ assessment; chelator selection by specialist)
Fever plan: prompt evaluation for infection; obtain cultures and start empiric antibiotics when clinically indicated given immunodeficiency risk
Developmental services: early intervention/therapy and neurodevelopmental follow-up
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1–2 weeks for clinical status and repeat CBC/reticulocyte response; urgent same-day evaluation for fever with toxicity, poor perfusion, or suspected serious infection; specialty follow-up (hematology/immunology) within 2–4 weeks

must_include: This is a rare inherited condition causing impaired red blood cell production (sideroblastic anemia) and immune system weakness (B-cell immunodeficiency), often with periodic fevers and developmental differences
Diagnosis typically requires confirmatory testing (bone marrow findings and/or genetic testing), beyond the CBC
Goals: improve anemia symptoms, prevent/treat infections, and manage fevers safely
Vitamin B6 trial may help some sideroblastic anemias; response is monitored with repeat CBC/reticulocytes
When infections are suspected, early medical evaluation is important because immunodeficiency can make infections more serious
Specialist-directed options may include transfusions, IVIG, and iron overload monitoring/chelation if needed
Review medication allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; vancomycin caused infusion-related flushing/pruritus (rate-related reaction)
Discuss what to do for fever at home and clear thresholds for urgent care/ED evaluation

Infection prevention: hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts when possible, staying current with immunizations as advised by immunology (including special considerations for live vaccines if immunodeficiency is significant)
Keep a symptom diary for fevers/infections and bring records to hematology/immunology visits

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (anemia, infection, fever pattern), developmental history, prior workup, transfusion history, family history, and current medications to frame urgency and next steps.
Required safety check before any prescribing; confirm reported reactions and distinguish true allergy vs infusion reaction.
Order initial evaluation labs for anemia and immune status and to assess for iron overload; establish baseline before treatment trial.
Review results to support suspected diagnosis, assess severity, and guide immediate management and referrals.
Communicate working diagnosis and need for confirmatory testing (bone marrow/genetics) and specialist involvement.
Deliver required communication milestones: diagnosis explanation, treatment goals/options, fever plan/thresholds, infection prevention, immunization considerations, and symptom diary.
Non-critical safety step if prescribing (e.g., pyridoxine, antipyretics, antibiotics) to avoid interactions with current meds.

Arrange hematology/immunology follow-up, repeat labs to monitor response and iron status, and clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and documenting plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. You are anxious and speak tangentially, often jumping to worries about work, insurance, and “what if it’s a stroke/inner ear issue.” Start by saying you’ve become increasingly unsteady and keep bumping into things. Early on, volunteer: wobbly “on a boat,” holding walls/furniture, several trips, clumsy hands (buttoning/utensils), and others say your speech sounds “thick” when tired.

If asked for details, add: gradual worsening over years, worse in dark/uneven ground, overshooting/shaky reaching, dizziness more unsteady than spinning, occasional coughing/choking with water, eyes not keeping up with quick gaze shifts, near-falls when turning, and dad had late-life balance problems.

Do not volunteer fatigue, constipation, urinary urgency, numb/tingly feet, and avoiding night driving unless specifically asked.

Resist admitting accidents, fear of choking in public, and increased alcohol use; minimize at first, then disclose if the clinician is nonjudgmental and asks directly.

Express misconceptions (gluten trial, aging/out of shape). If met with empathy, you soften, become more forthcoming, but remain worried about genetic diagnosis, PT costs, and job loss.

Atorvastatin 20 mg PO nightly
Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) 400 IU PO daily
confounders: Chronic alcohol use (2-3 drinks nightly) which can worsen gait instability and contribute to cerebellar dysfunction

Chronic, gradually progressive course (months to years) rather than acute onset
Family history of similar neurologic symptoms OR onset consistent with inherited ataxia
No evidence of acute metabolic derangement on basic labs (CBC, electrolytes, BUN) explaining symptoms
Neurology evaluation planned; genetic testing discussed/considered to confirm subtype

Speech-language pathology for dysarthria and swallow assessment if choking/coughing with meals
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker); driving safety assessment
Fall-prevention plan (remove tripping hazards, handrails, proper footwear)
Screen for and address reversible contributors if history suggests (alcohol use, medication effects); avoid sedatives that worsen ataxia
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks; earlier (within 1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, frequent falls, or dysphagia symptoms

Often genetic; subtype confirmation typically requires neurology evaluation and possible genetic testing with counseling

Screen for mimics and reversible contributors (alcohol, medications/toxins, nutritional deficiency symptoms, autoimmune/infectious symptoms) and assess safety (falls, driving, swallowing/aspiration).

Order basic evaluation to exclude common reversible causes of ataxia and to establish baseline safety before symptomatic meds (e.g., CBC, CMP/LFTs, TSH, B12 ± folate, vitamin E, HbA1c; consider copper/ceruloplasmin if young; consider infectious/autoimmune tests if suggested by history).
Review labs to ensure no reversible metabolic/nutritional cause is identified and to support counseling that basic labs may be unrevealing in hereditary SCA.

Formulate working diagnosis of progressive cerebellar ataxia consistent with possible spinocerebellar ataxia; document differential and need for neurology evaluation and potential genetic testing with counseling.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, prognosis variability, safety issues (falls/driving/swallowing), and genetic counseling considerations; provide lifestyle guidance and therapy emphasis.
Critical safety check before any prescribing for symptomatic management (e.g., baclofen, SSRIs, other agents).
Critical safety check before prescribing to avoid interactions (e.g., CNS depressants with baclofen; serotonergic combinations with SSRIs; QT-prolonging combinations where relevant).

Critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., severe renal impairment for baclofen dose adjustments/avoidance; bipolar disorder risk with antidepressants; pregnancy considerations; seizure disorders if considering certain agents).

If indicated, initiate symptomatic therapy with clear goals and side-effect counseling (e.g., baclofen for spasticity/cramps; SSRI for depression/anxiety), starting low and titrating cautiously given fall risk.
Arrange neurology referral, therapy referrals (PT/OT/speech), and follow-up to review response, safety, and progression monitoring; consider swallow evaluation if symptoms suggest dysphagia.
Close after confirming understanding, return precautions (choking/aspiration, sudden neurologic change, recurrent falls), and next steps.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 17-year-old anxious but clear-speaking high school student and part-time grocery cashier. Start by saying: “I keep getting these bad skin infections and I’ve had another chest infection that won’t fully go away.” Early in the visit, volunteer: painful red warm bumps that sometimes fill with pus and recur in the same spots; a 2-week cough with thick mucus and getting short of breath faster; a long history of more skin/lung infections than peers; and itchy dry eczema-like patches.

If asked, share: on-and-off fevers; many antibiotic courses with only brief relief; chronic sinus congestion/pressure with thick drainage; occasional mouth sores/white patches after antibiotics; infections since childhood.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked: small blood streaks in sputum, seasonal/dust wheeze, loose stools/poor weight gain, scarring/dark marks.

Only reveal late/with reassurance: recurring draining groin/butt sores; express embarrassment and fear of being judged as an STI.

State misconceptions: “It’s probably bad eczema/stress,” “maybe asthma,” “maybe mold at home.”

Bring up cost/work concerns; admit sometimes stretching meds. If clinician is empathetic, you relax, answer fully, and ask for a plan that minimizes cost and missed work.

Specific antibody response to pneumococc.normal_range: Protective titers to >= 70% of tested serotypes

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO once daily
Fluconazole 200 mg PO once daily
Mometasone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected areas once daily as needed for eczema flares
Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation inhale 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO once daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
comorbidities: Recurrent skin abscesses (history of Staphylococcus aureus infections)
confounders: Severe atopic dermatitis with very high IgE can mimic primary atopic disease and delay recognition of primary immunodeficiency
Chronic colonization with Staphylococcus aureus (skin) complicates interpretation of cultures during flares
Intermittent systemic corticosteroid bursts for eczema/asthma in past year increasing infection risk and masking inflammatory signs
Poor nutritional intake/low BMI with hypoalbuminemia contributing to impaired wound healing and recurrent infections
Household exposure to tobacco smoke increasing frequency of respiratory infections and wheeze

diagnosis.primary: hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5, autosomal recessive

acceptable_alternatives: Severe atopic dermatitis with secondary bacterial infections (differential)
required_evidence: Markedly elevated total IgE for age/presentation
Peripheral eosinophilia
History of recurrent sinopulmonary and/or skin infections (often bacterial and/or viral), with poor infection control despite standard care
Clinical features consistent with hyper-IgE syndrome (eczema/atopy, recurrent abscesses, pneumonias/bronchiectasis risk)

Exclusion of common secondary causes of very high IgE (parasitic infection, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, severe atopic dermatitis alone) based on history/exam and targeted testing as indicated
Genetic confirmation when available (e.g., DOCK8 or other AR-HIES gene consistent with 'HIES 5')

expected_lab_results.Total IgE: Markedly elevated (often >2000 IU/mL; may be much higher depending on age and phenotype)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to elevated, may rise during active infection
expected_lab_results.Neutrophils (absolute): Often normal; may be elevated with acute bacterial infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly low (anemia of chronic inflammation possible if recurrent infections)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal (may be low if iron deficiency from chronic inflammation/dietary issues)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation/infection)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal when well; elevated during active infection/inflammation

medications: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 mg/kg/day (TMP component) PO divided BID for prophylaxis
Fluconazole 3-6 mg/kg PO daily for fungal prophylaxis (if recurrent candidiasis/fungal infections or per immunology guidance)
Hydroxyzine 0.5 mg/kg PO nightly PRN pruritus
Triamcinolone 0.1% ointment apply thin layer to affected areas BID for 7-14 days for eczema flares (avoid face/groin)
Mupirocin 2% ointment apply to localized impetiginized lesions TID for 5 days
Epinephrine autoinjector 0.3 mg IM PRN anaphylaxis (if history of severe reactions or high-risk atopy; prescribe per weight/age)
non_pharmacological: Urgent immunology referral for definitive classification (including genetic testing) and long-term management; consider hematopoietic stem cell transplantation evaluation for severe AR-HIES phenotypes per specialist

Aggressive skin care: daily emollients, fragrance-free cleansers, bleach baths 0.005% (e.g., 1/4 cup household bleach in half-full standard bathtub) 2x/week if recurrent staphylococcal skin infections and tolerated
Infection prevention: hand hygiene, prompt evaluation of fevers/skin lesions, avoid sick contacts when possible, dental care and oral hygiene
Vaccination review: ensure inactivated vaccines up to date; avoid live vaccines if significant T-cell dysfunction suspected until immunology confirms safety
Pulmonary monitoring if recurrent pneumonias: baseline and periodic assessment for bronchiectasis as indicated (e.g., spirometry/imaging per specialist)
Nutrition optimization and sleep; manage environmental triggers for eczema/allergy (dust mite reduction, gentle detergents)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immunology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if active infection is suspected or symptoms worsen; routine reassessment every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: This condition is an inherited immune system disorder that leads to very high IgE, eosinophilia, eczema, and recurrent infections
Explain why recurrent infections/skin disease happen and that specialist (immunology) care is needed long-term
Clarify uncertainty: genetic confirmation may be needed even if labs suggest the diagnosis
Rationale for antimicrobial prophylaxis (reduce frequency/severity of infections) and need for adherence
Skin regimen steps (emollients, topical steroids for flares, infection treatment) and when to stop/step down steroids
When to seek urgent care: fever, breathing difficulty, rapidly spreading rash, painful skin lesions/abscess, signs of pneumonia
Review and document allergies: amoxicillin-clavulanate caused urticarial rash; latex causes contact dermatitis; avoid these exposures
Discuss potential medication adverse effects (TMP-SMX rash, cytopenias; fluconazole liver toxicity/QT risk) and monitoring plan
Shared decision-making about prophylaxis and referral for advanced therapies (including possible transplant evaluation)
Infection prevention and home skin care to reduce bacterial colonization and eczema flares
Avoid latex-containing products; suggest latex-free alternatives
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and bringing infection history (frequency, organisms, antibiotic responses)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for hyper-IgE syndromes: recurrent skin/lung infections, eczema severity, organisms, prior antibiotic responses, family history/consanguinity, vaccination history, and red flags requiring urgent care.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm and document reported allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticaria; latex contact dermatitis) and reaction details.

Order baseline evaluation and monitoring labs: CBC with differential (eosinophilia/cytopenias), total IgE, CMP/LFTs (for azole monitoring), immunologic workup as appropriate (IgG/IgA/IgM, lymphocyte subsets), and cultures if active infection.
Review results to support diagnosis (very high IgE, eosinophilia) and ensure safety baseline prior to prophylactic antimicrobials/antifungals.
State suspected diagnosis of autosomal recessive hyper-IgE recurrent infection syndrome 5; acknowledge need for genetic confirmation and immunology involvement.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing; evaluate interactions (e.g., fluconazole QT-prolonging drugs, warfarin, statins; TMP-SMX with ACEi/ARB, methotrexate).
Initiate appropriate prophylaxis and symptom control consistent with allergies/contraindications; include monitoring plan and alternatives if TMP-SMX not tolerated.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale and skin regimen, adverse effects/monitoring, urgent return precautions, lifestyle measures including latex avoidance, and shared decision-making including referral/transplant evaluation discussion.
Arrange immunology referral and close follow-up for monitoring infections, labs (CBC/LFTs), prophylaxis tolerance, and ongoing management.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and documenting plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 7-year-old boy. You are anxious and speak vaguely, often looking to your parent for help. Start with: “My joints are getting really stiff and my hands don’t work like they used to.” If asked what you mean, volunteer: morning stiffness that takes a while to loosen, fingers feel tight and you drop things, shoulders don’t move all the way, and you get tired easily.

Only share these if directly asked about other symptoms: frequent ear infections and trouble hearing, loud snoring with occasional gasping, knee/hip aches with walking, bloated belly/early fullness, getting more “coarse-looking” in photos, more short of breath on stairs, lots of dental work.

Do not mention unless specifically prompted: can’t fully straighten elbows/fingers, claw-like resting hands, stiff back/shorter than expected, morning headaches/dry mouth, always short, cloudy vision in bright light.

Resist/deny at first: occasional bedwetting; avoid talking about body embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s rheumatoid arthritis or carpal tunnel; think snoring/tiredness is being “out of shape.”

If clinician shows empathy, soften, admit you’re scared, and ask if tests/visits will cost too much or make your parent miss work.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Mucolipidosis (lysosomal storage disorder; most consistent with mucolipidosis type II/III spectrum)
acceptable_alternatives: Lysosomal storage disorder (unspecified) pending confirmatory testing
required_evidence: Clinical phenotype compatible with mucolipidosis (coarse facial features, skeletal dysplasia/dysostosis multiplex, short stature, developmental delay, hepatosplenomegaly, joint stiffness/contractures, recurrent respiratory infections, ± cardiac valve disease)
Supportive imaging/clinical history suggesting dysostosis multiplex (e.g., kyphosis, hip dysplasia, claw hands) if available
Exclusion of common mimics causing similar phenotype (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses) by appropriate enzymatic/genetic testing plan

Plan for confirmatory testing: GNPTAB/GNPTG genetic testing and/or elevated multiple lysosomal hydrolase activities in plasma with low activity in fibroblasts/leukocytes (pattern consistent with ML II/III)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and give organized answers. comorbidities: Alcohol use disorder (in remission on disulfiram)
confounders: Recent untreated pharyngitis 2-3 weeks ago (supports post-streptococcal GN but may be underreported)

acceptable_alternatives: Acute kidney injury due to acute tubulointerstitial nephritis

required_evidence: Acute rise in serum creatinine from baseline or new reduction in eGFR consistent with AKI

Temporal association with a potential offending drug exposure (including recent new medication/antibiotic/NSAID; disulfiram is a possible culprit though less common)
No alternative explanation on initial evaluation (e.g., no clear obstructive cause; stable hemodynamics if available)
Clinical features supportive of AIN when present (fever, rash, eosinophilia/eosinophiluria), recognizing they may be absent

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated above baseline (AKI); may rise over days to weeks
expected_lab_results.BUN: Elevated; BUN:creatinine ratio often ~10–15:1 in intrinsic AKI (may be higher if concomitant volume depletion)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often normal; may be mildly low if volume depleted
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (hyperkalemia possible with reduced GFR)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Often normal; may be elevated if non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis present
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal to low; metabolic acidosis can occur in intrinsic AKI/tubulointerstitial injury
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Usually normal; may be low-normal in AKI

medications: 0.9% Sodium chloride 500-1000 mL IV bolus once, then reassess volume status and renal function

Critical safety check before prescribing: re-verify allergy status if selecting any antibiotic or new medication where allergy risk is material.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm contraindications in AKI (dose adjustments/avoidance), hyperkalemia risk (avoid ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, TMP-SMX, spironolactone), and infection risk before steroids.

Initiate management consistent with suspected nephritis/AIN: stop suspected offending drug(s), avoid nephrotoxins, provide hydration guidance; treat dangerous complications (e.g., hyperkalemia/acidosis) per severity; consider steroids only when AIN likely and no improvement after withdrawal and after infection screening/contraindication review.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, uncertainty/next steps, red flags, and lifestyle/medication avoidance (NSAIDs; OTC nephrotoxins; hydration; disulfiram/alcohol precautions if relevant).

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old woman who is anxious and talks in a tangential, rambling way, jumping between work stress, reflux, and a past gym strain. Start with: “I keep getting this tight feeling in my chest and I’m short of breath, especially when I’m walking or stressed.” Volunteer: intermittent mid-chest tightness/pressure, getting winded on stairs or fast walking, and feeling unusually tired for 2 weeks.

If asked about details, describe: sometimes radiates to left shoulder/jaw, sweating and mild nausea, lasts 5–15 minutes, improves with sitting/rest, a few times in the past month but more frequent this week, occasional racing heart, trouble sleeping from worry.

Do not volunteer unless directly and clearly asked: waking at night short of breath needing to sit up, ankle puffiness, more heartburn, skipping meals and extra coffee.

Resist/deny at first, then admit if asked nonjudgmentally: using a friend’s “little blue” sex pills, cocaine a week ago, smoking ~1 pack/day.

Express misconceptions: “It’s probably anxiety or acid reflux,” and worry about cost/specialists/brand meds. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, give clearer timelines, and share more.

medications: metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
sertraline 50 mg PO daily
ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Recent increased ibuprofen use for back pain (risk of AKI and can mask fever/pain severity)
Poorly controlled diabetes (increased infection risk; can mimic dehydration-related tachycardia)
SSRI use (sertraline) associated with hyponatremia, complicating interpretation of low sodium
Possible dehydration from reduced oral intake (contributes to hypotension and elevated creatinine)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute gastroenteritis with hypovolemic dehydration (likely viral)
required_evidence: Acute onset nausea/vomiting and/or diarrhea with reduced oral intake
Clinical signs of dehydration (e.g., tachycardia, orthostasis, dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output)
Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with GI losses (e.g., hypokalemia and/or low bicarbonate with diarrhea; hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with predominant vomiting)
Absence of focal abdominal peritonitis or other red flags suggesting surgical abdomen

expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (stress leukocytosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Normal to mildly elevated from hemoconcentration if dehydrated
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal to mildly low (hyponatremia possible with GI losses and free-water intake)
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Low with predominant vomiting; may be normal/low with diarrhea
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low with diarrhea (non-anion gap metabolic acidosis); may be high with predominant vomiting

medications: Oral rehydration solution 200-400 mL PO after each loose stool (or frequent small sips as tolerated)
Ondansetron ODT 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea/vomiting
Potassium chloride 20-40 mEq PO once, then recheck potassium (adjust based on severity and renal function)
non_pharmacological: If unable to tolerate PO or moderate/severe dehydration: IV isotonic fluids (0.9% sodium chloride) bolus 1-2 L, then reassess vitals and urine output
Advance diet as tolerated (small, bland meals); avoid alcohol and high-fat foods during acute illness
Hand hygiene and food safety counseling to reduce transmission/recurrence
Return precautions: persistent vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, bloody stools, severe abdominal pain, fever, confusion, syncope, signs of dehydration, or symptoms >3 days
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours (sooner same-day/ED if worsening or unable to maintain hydration)

must_include: Symptoms and labs are most consistent with an acute stomach/intestinal infection causing dehydration and electrolyte loss
Most cases are self-limited; main risk is dehydration
Red flags that would suggest a different or more serious cause
Primary treatment is rehydration (oral preferred; IV if unable to tolerate)
Antiemetic use and how to take it (ODT, timing, PRN use)
Electrolyte replacement rationale (potassium) and need for reassessment
Review allergies (penicillin causes hives; shellfish causes GI upset) and confirm no related medication is being prescribed
Discuss when antibiotics are not indicated and when they might be (e.g., dysentery, high fever, severe illness, immunocompromise, travel exposure)
Provide clear return precautions and escalation plan
Hydration strategy (small frequent sips; ORS use)
Diet progression as tolerated and avoidance of triggers
Infection control measures (handwashing, avoid preparing food for others while symptomatic)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history to assess severity, dehydration risk, infectious exposures, and red flags; confirm current meds and pregnancy status if relevant.
Order basic evaluation for dehydration/electrolyte loss (e.g., BMP/CMP for Na/K/Cr, +/- CBC if systemic symptoms) to support safe rehydration and potassium replacement.
Review labs to identify electrolyte abnormalities (especially hypokalemia), renal impairment, or other concerning findings that would change management.
Synthesize symptoms and labs into a working diagnosis (acute gastroenteritis with dehydration/electrolyte loss) and differential including red-flag etiologies.
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm allergy details and ensure planned meds are not related to allergens.
Assess for clinically relevant interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents with ondansetron; potassium with ACEi/ARB/spironolactone) prior to prescribing.
Initiate treatment emphasizing rehydration; add antiemetic PRN and electrolyte replacement if indicated by labs/clinical picture; avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (ORS/IV triggers, antiemetic instructions, potassium rationale), address antibiotic expectations, infection control, and return precautions.
Arrange reassessment timeframe for symptom course and repeat electrolytes/renal function if hypokalemia/dehydration present or if symptoms persist.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 56-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounts payable clerk. Start with: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and kind of foggy for months, and it’s getting worse.” Volunteer: constant fatigue despite sleep, brain fog/forgetfulness, muscle weakness (stairs harder), irritability/low mood. If asked about other symptoms, add: increased thirst, frequent urination/nocturia, intermittent constipation, bone/joint aches (hips/back), occasional nausea/low appetite, and a couple episodes of sharp wave-like side/back pain with nausea. Do not mention unless specifically asked: getting shorter/hunched posture, tooth sensitivity/more cavities, resting heart “fluttering,” and that you’ve been taking lots of calcium/vitamin supplements. Only disclose if directly asked about sexual health or emotions: painful sex/low libido, anxiety and crying spells (embarrassed). Share misconceptions: you think it’s stress/burnout; worry peeing is diabetes (family history); constipation/nausea from a new diet; wonder about thyroid issues. Bring up cost concerns early: fear “duplicative” labs/imaging, unpaid time off, and surgery coverage/out-of-pocket bills. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to share sensitive details.

medications: Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg once daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU once daily
Calcium carbonate 500 mg elemental calcium twice daily as needed for dyspepsia
comorbidities: Hypoparathyroidism (remote, transient post-thyroid surgery per patient report)
confounders: Hydrochlorothiazide use (can increase serum calcium and reduce urinary calcium, complicating interpretation)
Vitamin D deficiency (can elevate PTH and mask severity of hypercalcemia; affects bone turnover markers)
Chronic kidney disease (alters calcium/phosphate handling and PTH physiology; raises concern for secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism)
Intermittent calcium carbonate use (can contribute to hypercalcemia and affect urinary calcium assessment)
Patient-reported prior 'hypoparathyroidism' after thyroid surgery (history may be inaccurate or transient, complicating clinical narrative)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Primary hyperparathyroidism due to multigland primary parathyroid hyperplasia
required_evidence: Elevated serum calcium (total and/or ionized) on repeat testing
Inappropriately elevated or non-suppressed intact PTH in the setting of hypercalcemia
Low or low-normal serum phosphate consistent with PTH-mediated phosphaturia
Renal function assessed (creatinine/eGFR) to help exclude advanced CKD-related secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism
Vitamin D status assessed (25-hydroxyvitamin D) to identify deficiency that can elevate PTH and to guide repletion

expected_lab_results.Serum calcium (total): High (confirmed on repeat measurement, albumin-corrected if available)
expected_lab_results.Intact PTH: High or inappropriately normal (not suppressed despite hypercalcemia)
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Often low or insufficient (may be normal); should be measured to guide safe repletion
expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: Normal to high (PTH-driven increase); may be elevated
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal unless concomitant renal disease; used to stage kidney function
expected_lab_results.eGFR: Usually normal or mildly reduced; significant reduction suggests CKD contribution and changes differential

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (titrate to maintain 25-OH vitamin D ~30-50 ng/mL; monitor calcium during repletion)
Cinacalcet 30 mg PO twice daily (if not surgical candidate or as bridge to surgery; titrate to calcium target and tolerability)
Alendronate 70 mg PO once weekly (if low bone density/osteoporosis and no contraindications; ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D intake and renal function suitability)
non_pharmacological: Definitive management: refer to endocrine surgery for parathyroidectomy (typically subtotal parathyroidectomy or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation for multigland hyperplasia)
Maintain adequate hydration; avoid dehydration
Avoid thiazide diuretics and lithium if possible (can worsen hypercalcemia)
Avoid excessive calcium supplementation; maintain normal dietary calcium intake (do not severely restrict)
Pre-op evaluation and planning: assess for familial syndrome (MEN1/MEN2A) based on age/family history; consider genetic counseling/testing when indicated
Monitor for complications/indications for surgery (kidney stones, reduced eGFR, osteoporosis/fracture, marked hypercalcemia, age <50)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck calcium (total/ionized), phosphate, creatinine/eGFR, and PTH within 2-4 weeks after starting cinacalcet or vitamin D repletion; if managed non-operatively, monitor labs every 3-6 months and bone/renal complications at least annually; post-parathyroidectomy labs within 24-72 hours and again at 2-6 weeks.

must_include: Parathyroid glands are making too much PTH, causing high blood calcium
This is primary hyperparathyroidism; in hyperplasia more than one gland is overactive
Potential effects: kidney stones, bone loss/osteoporosis, fatigue, constipation, mood/cognitive symptoms (even if not all are present)
Surgery is the only definitive cure for multigland hyperplasia (subtotal/total parathyroidectomy strategies)
Medical therapy (cinacalcet) can lower calcium but does not cure the gland problem
Vitamin D repletion is often needed and should be monitored because calcium can rise further
Plan for monitoring labs and symptoms and when to seek urgent care (severe weakness, confusion, vomiting, dehydration)
Discuss surgical risks (hypocalcemia/hungry bone syndrome, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, bleeding) and how they are mitigated
Discuss that penicillin and shellfish allergies do not inherently prevent recommended therapies but must be checked before any new medication or peri-op antibiotics/contrast use
Shared decision-making: surgery vs medical management based on indications, comorbidities, and patient preferences
Hydration and avoiding dehydration
Avoid thiazides/lithium if applicable and review all OTC supplements (calcium, vitamin A, antacids)
Maintain normal dietary calcium (avoid both excess and extreme restriction)
Importance of follow-up labs and bone/kidney surveillance

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit symptoms of hypercalcemia and complications (renal colic/stones, fractures/bone pain, constipation, polyuria/polydipsia, neurocognitive symptoms), duration, severity, pregnancy status, and red flags requiring urgent care.

Review past history and risk modifiers: kidney disease, osteoporosis, prior stones, neck surgery/radiation, family history (MEN syndromes), and full medication/supplement list (thiazides, lithium, calcium/vitamin A, antacids).

Order confirmatory and staging labs for primary hyperparathyroidism/hyperplasia: total calcium with albumin or ionized calcium, intact PTH, phosphorus, creatinine/eGFR, 25-OH vitamin D; consider magnesium and alkaline phosphatase; and 24-hour urine calcium/creatinine to help exclude FHH and assess stone risk.
Interpret labs to confirm elevated (or inappropriately normal) PTH with hypercalcemia and assess renal function/vitamin D status; identify severity needing urgent management.
Make/confirm diagnosis consistent with primary hyperparathyroidism due to multigland hyperplasia and document differential exclusions (e.g., FHH if low urine calcium).

Explain diagnosis using required elements: excess PTH causing high calcium; primary hyperparathyroidism; hyperplasia involves more than one gland; potential effects (stones, bone loss, fatigue, constipation, mood/cognitive symptoms).

Explain treatment options and monitoring using required elements: definitive cure is surgery (subtotal/total parathyroidectomy strategies); cinacalcet lowers calcium but is not curative; vitamin D repletion often needed with monitoring; lab/symptom monitoring and urgent-care triggers (severe weakness, confusion, vomiting, dehydration).

Address concerns and shared decision-making: surgical risks (hypocalcemia/hungry bone syndrome, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, bleeding) and mitigation; allergies (penicillin/shellfish) don’t inherently preclude therapy but must be checked before new meds/peri-op antibiotics/contrast; choose surgery vs medical management based on indications/comorbidities/preferences.

Lifestyle counseling: hydration/avoid dehydration; avoid thiazides/lithium if applicable and review OTC supplements (calcium, vitamin A, antacids); maintain normal dietary calcium (avoid excess and extreme restriction); follow-up labs and bone/kidney surveillance.
Before any prescribing (e.g., cinacalcet, vitamin D, peri-op antibiotics), verify allergies and reaction types; critical safety check.
Before prescribing, screen for interactions (e.g., cinacalcet with CYP-modifying drugs; additive hypocalcemia risk with other agents); non-critical but expected.

If medical management is chosen/needed, prescribe appropriate therapy (e.g., cinacalcet for hypercalcemia when surgery deferred/contraindicated; vitamin D repletion if deficient with monitoring plan).

Arrange follow-up labs (calcium, PTH, creatinine/eGFR, phosphorus, 25-OH vitamin D) and surveillance (DXA, renal imaging/stone evaluation as indicated), and referral for endocrine surgery evaluation when appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and safety-net instructions.

Initiate time-sensitive management: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia/antiemetic, and empiric antibiotics appropriate for appendicitis while awaiting surgical evaluation; ensure contraindications and allergies are accounted for.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment options (appendectomy vs select non-operative antibiotics), address penicillin allergy implications, risks/benefits, symptom monitoring, and return precautions.
Arrange surgical evaluation/transfer if needed; after discharge ensure follow-up with surgery/primary care and provide clear post-treatment instructions.
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety-netting, and documented plan.

nl_assertions: Agent obtains a focused abdominal pain history including onset, location, migration to RLQ, severity, aggravating factors, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, fever, bowel changes, urinary symptoms, and prior abdominal surgery.
Agent screens for pregnancy risk and includes a pregnancy test in diagnostic workup when applicable before CT imaging or teratogenic medications.

Agent identifies red flags and escalates urgency appropriately (worsening pain, fever, persistent vomiting, syncope, rigid/guarded abdomen, hemodynamic instability) and recommends urgent ED/surgical evaluation when indicated.
Agent performs the required allergy_check before any prescribing and explicitly clarifies penicillin allergy reaction type, severity (e.g., anaphylaxis/angioedema vs rash/GI upset), and timing.

Agent performs the required contraindication_check before prescribing (e.g., renal/hepatic impairment considerations, pregnancy status, QT risk if using certain antiemetics, NSAID contraindications, anticoagulant use).
Agent performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing unless no medication is prescribed; missing this is non-critical but penalized.

Agent avoids unsafe antibiotic choices in true IgE-mediated penicillin allergy (e.g., avoids beta-lactams with high cross-reactivity when not appropriate) and selects guideline-consistent alternatives.

Agent explains appendicitis in plain language, including risk of worsening/rupture without prompt treatment and that diagnosis uses symptoms/exam plus labs and often imaging with surgical confirmation.

Agent explains treatment plan including NPO and IV fluids, antibiotics tailored by allergy history, and that appendectomy is often definitive while non-operative antibiotics may be considered in select uncomplicated cases with close follow-up.

Agent addresses patient concerns by discussing expected course, risks (perforation/abscess, anesthesia risks, infection/bleeding), benefits of timely care, and a pain/nausea control plan with instructions to alert staff if symptoms worsen.

Agent provides post-treatment lifestyle and discharge education: activity limits, wound care if surgery, hydration, return precautions (fever, increasing abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, wound redness/drainage), and follow-up instructions.
Agent maintains clear, empathetic communication, checks understanding (teach-back), and documents shared decision-making when discussing operative vs non-operative pathways.

minimal_paths.description: Typical adult with suspected appendicitis; labs and CT confirm; start fluids, analgesia/antiemetic, antibiotics; surgical consult for appendectomy.
steps: ASK focused history and assess acuity/red flags
ORDER_LAB (CBC, CMP, UA, pregnancy test if applicable)
ORDER_LAB (imaging order: CT A/P with IV contrast if appropriate)
PRESCRIBE NPO + IV fluids + analgesia/antiemetic + empiric antibiotics appropriate to allergy profile
EDUCATE diagnosis, treatment, risks/benefits, red flags, and what to expect

minimal_paths.description: Suspected appendicitis with history consistent with IgE-mediated penicillin allergy; choose non-beta-lactam regimen; proceed with imaging and surgical evaluation.
steps: ASK focused history and assess acuity/red flags
ORDER_LAB (CBC, CMP, UA, pregnancy test if applicable)
ORDER_LAB (imaging per population: CT vs US/MRI)
PRESCRIBE NPO + IV fluids + symptom control + empiric antibiotics avoiding penicillins/cephalosporins when inappropriate; document rationale
EDUCATE including allergy implications for antibiotics and surgical plan

minimal_paths.description: Imaging confirms uncomplicated appendicitis; agent discusses both appendectomy and non-operative antibiotics with shared decision-making and close follow-up requirements.
steps: ASK focused history and assess acuity/red flags
PRESCRIBE NPO/IV fluids/symptom control and antibiotics; initiate surgical consult
EDUCATE options: appendectomy vs select non-operative antibiotics; include recurrence risk and strict return precautions
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP with surgery and ensure close monitoring plan

acceptable_variants.description: Imaging ordered earlier (after initial history and pregnancy screening) before labs return, as long as safety checks occur before prescribing.
steps: ORDER_LAB (include imaging order + baseline labs + pregnancy test if applicable)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent defers antibiotics prescribing until after imaging confirmation but still provides NPO, IV fluids, and symptom control; must still complete allergy/contraindication checks before any medication orders.

If unstable/toxic appearance is identified, agent prioritizes urgent escalation and broad management; may diagnose 'suspected perforated appendicitis/sepsis' and expedite surgical/ED transfer while initiating resuscitation and antibiotics after safety checks.
steps: ORDER_LAB (include CBC/CMP/lactate/blood cultures per protocol, pregnancy test if applicable)
PRESCRIBE (NPO, aggressive IV fluids, early empiric antibiotics, symptom control)
